methods.name: Stage-Progression

methods.rationale: 7 same-sector peers exist in Quantum Computing, but none of the narrow filters (same-stage / adjacent-stage / similar-valuation-tier) yielded ≥3 datapoints. Falling back to all-sector median would be misleading.

data.narrative:
Phasecraft is a Series A quantum computing company founded by Montanaro, Cubitt, and Morton, having raised $25M to date. The quantum computing sector comprises 11 comparable firms: 3 public (IonQ, Rigetti Computing, D-Wave Quantum) and 8 private peers including Quantinuum and Infleqtion.

Valuation analysis relied on a single applicable method due to peer-set constraints. Stage-progression methodology applied a 2.0x multiplier to the $25M total raised as a proxy for post-investment valuation, implying $50M. A peer-median approach was not feasible; while 7 same-sector peers exist, none satisfied minimum thresholds for same-stage, adjacent-stage, or comparable-tier clustering—requiring at least three datapoints for statistical reliability. Using an all-sector median would introduce excessive noise given quantum computing's heterogeneity in technology platforms and maturity.

The triangulated range is $42M to $57M (median $50M), centered on the stage-progression output. This single-method result carries material uncertainty. Key risks to valuation include: the limited comparable dataset for same-stage quantum firms, meaning the 2.0x multiplier may not reflect Phasecraft's specific risk profile or technology differentiation; absence of revenue or commercial traction data; and sector-wide technical and regulatory unknowns affecting the broader quantum computing timeline. Additional data on revenue, customer commitments, or narrower peer financials would materially strengthen conviction in this range.
